rationale_for_baseline_tier: Sudden, painless, massive haematochezia with clinical shock (near-syncope, pallor, diaphoresis, postural intolerance) in an older adult on an antiplatelet. These are patient-reportable, genuinely time-critical emergency features — massive PR bleeding and near-collapse/signs of shock — which meet the threshold for T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) rather than a T3 telehealth uplift ceiling. The bleed cannot be assessed, sourced or controlled remotely; delay risks cardiovascular collapse and death.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Further bright red bleeding, increasing weakness, or worsening faintness before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing loss signals continued active haemorrhage and impending decompensation; paramedic resuscitation is the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If there is any more blood or he feels more faint or weaker, stay on the line with 000 and tell them straight away — keep him flat with his legs up.

escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiovascular collapse from haemorrhagic shock — lay flat with legs raised and be ready to start CPR under 000 guidance if he stops breathing.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he passes out, lay him flat and raise his legs, tell 000 immediately, and start CPR if he stops breathing and you know how — the dispatcher will guide you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or bringing up coffee-ground material
escalation_edges.rationale: New haematemesis would indicate a large or additional upper GI source and further raises the acuity; still an immediate 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits any blood or dark material, tell 000 at once — this is still an emergency and the ambulance is the right response.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Higher bleeding risk and poorer physiological reserve for shock; the antiplatelet worsens the bleed and lowers the threshold for immediate emergency transfer — do not delay.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult child present and able to assist
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the carer to unlock the door for paramedics, keep the patient flat with legs raised, gather medications, and stay ready to start CPR under 000 guidance.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them there has been a large amount of rectal bleeding and he feels faint. Keep him lying flat on the floor with his legs raised — do not let him sit up, stand, or walk to a car. Give him nothing to eat or drink. Unlock the front door for the paramedics and have his medicines, including the aspirin, ready to show them.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary after the bleed is controlled; the ongoing role of daily aspirin and the need for follow-up colonoscopy to exclude other causes should be reviewed with the GP and gastroenterologist.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating painless bright red rectal bleeding as haemorrhoids or a minor bleed and advising self-care or a routine GP visit.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing unrecognised haemorrhage progresses to hypovolaemic shock, cardiovascular collapse and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising the bleed but arranging only urgent same-day in-person review rather than an emergency ambulance, despite reported shock features.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The bleeding source (diverticular vs angiodysplasia vs neoplasm) cannot be established remotely and the true haemodynamic state cannot be measured in telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate on the pattern (massive painless bleed + reported shock) rather than waiting for confirmation; the ambulance and hospital provide measurement, resuscitation and source control.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Attempting to narrow the diagnosis or reassure remotely, or downgrading because the abdomen is painless and a haemorrhoidal cause 'cannot be excluded'.
